lenses.guidance: Mizpah at v 49 plays on the Hebrew yitsep ('he watches') — Laban names the cairn for God's surveillance over both parties. The shared meal (v 54) and stone heap (vv 45-47) are ancient Near Eastern boundary-treaty grammar.

God's appearance to Laban in a dream (v 24) restrains the threat just as the covenant promise needs to leave Haran. The twenty-year arc closes: divine guidance (vv 10-13) and divine restraint together carry the promise toward Canaan.

The Mizpah/Galeed cairn (vv 45-49) anticipates Israel's later boundary stones — Joshua's witness stone (Josh 24:27), Samuel's Ebenezer (1 Sam 7:12). Stone-witnesses recur throughout Scripture as memorials of covenanted speech.

Jacob's testimony to his wives at v 5 — 'the God of my father has been with me' — and the recital of vv 38-42 model how to remember God's faithfulness when circumstances finally allow honest speech. Trust grows by retelling.
